input:
ALICE: Okay, so we need to decide on the Q3 roadmap.
BOB: I think we should prioritize the API refactor.
CAROL: Wait, can we talk about the mobile app first? Our users are complaining.
ALICE: Right, so mobile is important, but the API affects everything.
DAVID: Actually, I wanted to bring up the hiring plan...
BOB: Can we finish one thing? We were talking about the API.
CAROL: I don't think we agreed on that. I never agreed to that.
ALICE: [45 minutes later] Let's just table this and meet again next week.
[Everyone agrees, no decision made]

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Never established shared definition of Q3 success
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - No pre-read, no shared data on user complaints vs technical debt
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No agenda, no motion on floor, no recognition of speakers, interruptions allowed
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear if Alice as PM has authority to decide or if this is consensus
stack_assessment.level_5_decision: ✗ - No decision captured, just 'table and meet again'

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Set agenda with decision items, use motions ('I move we prioritize...'), recognize speakers, require seconds, limit debate with Previous Question

input: The VP of Engineering and the VP of Product both think they have final say on technical roadmap decisions. This has been going on for 6 months. Engineering keeps building things Product didn't prioritize, and Product keeps adding requirements that Engineering says they never agreed to. The CEO says 'work it out between yourselves.' Both report to the CEO. There's no documented RACI.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Engineering goals (clean architecture) vs Product goals (feature velocity)
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Each side doesn't know what the other committed to
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for who decides what
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Complete collision, both claim same decision rights
stack_assessment.level_5_decision: ✗ - Decisions made unilaterally by each, not coordinated

expected_diagnosis.prescription: CEO must clarify decision rights (RACI), document which decisions each VP owns, establish joint decision process for overlapping areas

input: Our cross-functional project team just doesn't seem to gel. Everyone nods in meetings but then nothing happens. I don't know what's wrong.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May have competing departmental goals not surfaced
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ? - Information may not be reaching all members
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - 'Nodding in meetings' suggests no real process for surfacing dissent
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear who can hold people accountable

root_causes: Multiple levels - Needs probing to determine
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Conduct 1:1s to surface real concerns, check for competing incentives, ensure decisions documented with owners

input:
DECISION LOG:
Jan 15: Board votes 7-2 to move annual gala to November (was June)
Feb 3: Marketing committee discusses - some members weren't at Jan meeting, question the change
Feb 20: Board reopens discussion, some members say 'we never really decided'
Mar 1: Vote again, 5-4 to keep November date
Mar 15: Development director says 'the board keeps changing their mind, I can't plan'
Apr 1: Three board members who missed Mar 1 meeting want to revisit again

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Fundraising vs donor availability vs board availability conflicts
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Board members not at meetings not getting proper info
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for reconsideration, no standard for when to reopen
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - May be confusion about board vs committee authority
stack_assessment.level_5_decision: ✗ - Decisions not captured properly, minutes don't show 7-2 vote clearly, absentees not informed

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Record minutes with vote counts, notify absent board members of decisions, establish 2/3 threshold for reconsideration within 3 months

input: The leadership team has been discussing the acquisition for 3 months. We have a data room, we've done 5 presentations, but we can't decide. Half the team thinks we need more analysis, half thinks we have enough. We keep asking the CFO for more scenarios.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May not agree on acquisition strategy
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Information asymmetry, some want more data than others
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No deadline, no process for determining 'enough' information
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear if CEO decides or if this is consensus

root_causes: Level 3 - No process to close debate
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Set decision deadline, use Previous Question to close debate, clarify who decides, define 'sufficient information' standard

input:
CHAIR: Let's discuss the cloud migration budget.
CTO: We need $2M for AWS reserved instances.
CFO: That's not in the annual budget we approved.
CTO: But the technical requirements changed.
CFO: Any spend over $500K needs board approval.
CTO: That rule is for new initiatives, this is existing infrastructure.
CFO: Infrastructure overruns still need approval.
CTO: I've already committed to the vendor.
CFO: You what? Without approval?

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - CTO goal (technical excellence) vs CFO goal (budget control)
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - CTO didn't inform CFO before committing
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - CTO bypassed approval process
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Unclear if CTO has spending authority or needs CFO/board
stack_assessment.level_5_decision: ✗ - CTO made unilateral decision without authority

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Clarify spending authority in bylaws/policy, establish procurement process, align CTO/CFO on shared goals

input: We have 'disagree and commit' as a value but nobody actually commits. Every decision gets re-argued in Slack for days after the meeting. The CEO makes a call, half the team agrees publicly, then keeps debating privately.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May not actually agree on 'disagree and commit'
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Private Slack debates show decisions not communicated properly
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for closing debate and committing
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✓ - CEO makes call, authority is clear

root_causes: Level 3 - No process to end debate and enforce commitment
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Use Previous Question to close debate, document decisions with 'final' status, establish no-reopening period

input:
PM: We need to ship this feature by Friday.
ENGINEERING LEAD: That's not enough time for proper testing.
PM: The client committed to their customer.
ENGINEERING LEAD: I won't release untested code.
PM: Are you refusing a direct business requirement?
ENGINEERING LEAD: I'm refusing to compromise engineering standards.
PM: This is a revenue issue. The CEO said revenue is priority one.
ENGINEERING LEAD: And I'm responsible for system stability.
PM: Who's in charge here?
ENGINEERING LEAD: Good question.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Revenue vs quality goals in conflict
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Client commitment made without engineering input
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for resolving PM/eng conflicts
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Collision: PM thinks they can set deadlines, Eng thinks they control release

root_causes: Level 4 - Unclear authority between PM and Engineering Lead
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Document decision rights for product vs engineering, establish escalation path to common authority

input:
DECISION LOG:
Q1: Team decides to use React for new project
Q2: New architect joins, questions decision, team switches to Vue
Q3: Another senior dev joins, argues for Svelte, switches again
Q4: Original React advocate returns from leave, wants to revisit
Current: Team has rewritten same components 3 times, no shipping

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Shipping vs 'best' technology
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - New people don't have context on original decision rationale
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No bar for reconsideration, new person can reopen any decision
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear who has final tech authority

root_causes: Level 2 - Information loss when people join/leave
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Document decision rationale (ADRs), require new evidence to reconsider, set decision permanence period

input: Our board keeps having the same conversation about the strategic plan. We 'agreed' on it 6 months ago but we're still discussing whether it's the right direction. Meanwhile, staff can't execute because they don't know what's actually decided.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May have goal disagreement not surfaced
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Staff don't have clear communication of decisions
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ? - Process unclear for when plan is 'final'
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear who can stop the revisiting
stack_assessment.level_5_decision: ✗ - 'Agreed' but no documented, final decision captured

root_causes: Level 5 - Decision not captured as final
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Document strategic plan as board resolution with vote, communicate to staff, set review date (not continuous)

input: We have great discussions in meetings but we never seem to reach conclusions. The founder likes to explore all angles and won't cut off debate. Two hours later everyone's exhausted and we schedule another meeting.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✓ - Generally aligned
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✓ - Good discussion suggests info sharing
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No closure mechanism, founder won't use Previous Question
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✓ - Founder has authority

root_causes: Level 3 - No process to close debate
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Set meeting time boxes, assign someone to move Previous Question, use voting to force closure

input:
VP: We need to decide on the vendor by EOW.
DIRECTOR A: I've analyzed Vendor X - they're cheapest.
DIRECTOR B: But Vendor Y has better security.
DIRECTOR C: What about Vendor Z? Nobody evaluated them.
VP: We don't have time for Vendor Z.
DIRECTOR C: But what if Z is better?
DIRECTOR A: Can we get an extension?
VP: The RFP deadline is fixed.
DIRECTOR B: I need more security documentation from X.
DIRECTOR A: That'll take 2 weeks.
[Cycle continues for 6 weeks]

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Cost vs security vs thoroughness
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Incomplete information (Vendor Z), conflicting criteria
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for determining 'enough' analysis
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Directors can block without accountability

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Define decision criteria upfront, set information cutoff date, clarify who decides, use vote to close

input: Sales keeps promising features to customers that Product hasn't prioritized. Engineering says the roadmap is 'a suggestion, not a commitment.' Marketing announces launch dates that Engineering hasn't agreed to. Everyone blames everyone else.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Sales (revenue) vs Product (strategy) vs Engineering (quality) vs Marketing (launch) goals conflict
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Commitments made without coordination
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for cross-functional commitment
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Each function claims authority to make commitments

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Align on shared goals (revenue + sustainable delivery), create roadmap as joint decision, establish commitment protocol

input: Our executive director and board chair keep stepping on each other's toes. The ED thinks they run the organization. The board chair thinks they oversee the ED. Staff get conflicting directions.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May agree on mission but different approaches
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Staff getting conflicting info
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for resolving ED/Board conflicts
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Bylaws don't clearly define ED vs Board Chair roles

root_causes: Level 4 - Unclear authority between ED and Board Chair
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Review and amend bylaws to clarify ED vs Board authority, document decision rights, establish governance committee

input:
DECISION LOG:
Week 1: Team decides to focus on enterprise customers
Week 2: One engineer builds SMB feature anyway 'because it's easy'
Week 3: Founder sees SMB feature, gets excited, pivots discussion to SMB
Week 4: Team 'reconsiders' enterprise focus
Week 5: Sales says they have 3 enterprise deals waiting, why did we pivot?
[Pattern repeats every 4-6 weeks for 2 years]

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Enterprise vs SMB - never fully committed
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Founder not getting consistent info
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - Engineer can bypass decision, Founder can reopen without process
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear if team decision binds everyone

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Document strategic decisions, require explicit authorization to deviate, enforce consequences for bypassing

input:
CEO: Should we enter the European market?
CFO: I've run the numbers - NPV positive in year 3.
CMO: But we don't know the competitive landscape.
CEO: What do we need to know?
CMO: Market research, competitor analysis, partner evaluation...
CTO: And technical infrastructure for GDPR.
CEO: How long will all that take?
CMO: 6-9 months.
CFO: But delaying costs us $2M in first-mover advantage.
[90 minutes of debating whether to research more or decide now]

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - Growth vs caution - may not be aligned
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Uncertainty about what's 'enough' information
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for determining information sufficiency
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear if CEO decides or this is consensus

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Define 'minimum viable information' for market entry, set decision deadline, clarify who decides

input: Our town halls have become unproductive. 200 people, anyone can ask anything, the CEO tries to answer everything, and 2 hours later nobody remembers what was decided. The agenda is always 'updates and Q&A.'

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✓ - Generally aligned on business
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✓ - Updates provide info
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No structure, no motions, no decisions, Q&A only
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - Unclear what CEO can decide vs what needs consensus

root_causes: Level 3 - No decision-making process in town halls
expected_diagnosis.prescription: Separate info-sharing (updates) from decision-making, add decision items to agenda, use structured format

input:
LEAD: Let's align on the QOKRs.
ENG: Our objective is zero critical bugs.
SALES: Ours is hit $5M ARR.
PRODUCT: Ours is NPS > 50.
ENG: Those aren't aligned.
SALES: They're all important.
PRODUCT: Can't we have all three?
ENG: You're asking us to prioritize bug fixes AND ship features for $5M ARR. That's incompatible.
SALES: [later, to team] Just hit the number, quality can wait.
ENG: [later, to team] Ship clean code, ignore sales pressure.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ✗ - Zero bugs vs $5M ARR vs NPS - fundamentally incompatible without prioritization
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - Teams working from different priorities
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - QOKR process allows incompatible objectives
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ? - No one forced prioritization

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Force rank objectives, establish which takes precedence when in conflict, align incentives

input: We've been 'about to' launch for 6 months. Every time we're close, someone finds a reason we need to fix something first. The founders can't agree on what's 'good enough.'

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May disagree on launch criteria
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ✗ - No shared definition of 'good enough'
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No process for determining launch readiness
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Multiple founders, unclear who decides

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Define MVP criteria upfront, establish launch decision process, clarify founder decision rights

input: We have a 'two-in-a-box' leadership model. The Engineering Director and Product Director are supposed to co-lead. But they disagree constantly and neither can overrule the other. Escalations go to their VP who says 'work it out.' Their teams are caught in the middle.

stack_assessment.level_1_goals: ? - May have goal conflicts
stack_assessment.level_2_information: ? - Teams may have asymmetric info
stack_assessment.level_3_process: ✗ - No tiebreaker mechanism
stack_assessment.level_4_authority: ✗ - Designed to have no single authority, escalation fails

expected_diagnosis.prescription: Restructure to single authority, or define explicit tiebreaker process, clarify escalation to VP
